news_summary: Today's positive news highlights economic resilience despite demographic changes, the success of diverse voices overcoming adversity, and a renewed enthusiasm for language learning in education.
news_summary_fr: Les bonnes nouvelles d'aujourd'hui mettent en évidence la résilience économique malgré les changements démographiques, la réussite de diverses voix qui ont surmonté l'adversité, ainsi qu'un regain d'enthousiasme pour l'apprentissage des langues dans le domaine de l'éducation.
news_summary_es: Las noticias positivas de hoy ponen de relieve la resiliencia económica a pesar de los cambios demográficos, el éxito de voces diversas que superan la adversidad y un renovado entusiasmo por el aprendizaje de idiomas en el ámbito educativo.

articles.title: Why lower fertility does not have to mean economic decline
articles.summary: Globally, most people say they want two or more children, but many are having only one, or none at all. According to a senior UN economist, fears of a demographic timebomb are unwarranted.
articles.content:
There’s no getting away from the fact that in many countries around the world, populations are ageing and fertility is declining.

The latest State of World Population report from the UN sexual and reproductive rights agency (UNFPA) shows that around one in five adults worldwide believe they will not be able to have the number of children they want, largely because of economic insecurity, inequality and lack of support.

But Michael Herrmann, an economist and demographer with UNFPA, cautions against panic. “Demographic change is not a crisis in itself,” he says. “It’s a reality we need to understand, plan for, and adapt to.”

UN News Michael Herrmann, UNFPA adviser on economics and demography.

Demographic resilience

Mr. Herrmann, who spoke on the sidelines of the Commission on Population and Development, which is meeting this week at UN Headquarters in New York, is advocating for a concept that is gaining attention: demographic resilience.

This means helping societies to anticipate population change, adapt their institutions and make better use of their human potential – an approach that is applicable to developing and wealthy countries, whether their populations are growing, shrinking, or ageing.

Some countries experience a “demographic dividend” when a growing working-age population boosts economic growth.

Others, further along the demographic transition, can benefit from a “second dividend” by investing in education, health, skills and technology to raise productivity.

Honey, I shrunk the workforce

One of the most visible effects of ageing populations is a shrinking workforce. Many governments have responded by raising retirement ages, a response that Herrmann says is often too blunt an instrument.

Simply requiring everyone to work longer ignores the different capacities, preferences and life circumstances of older adults.

Some may want to keep working, albeit in part-time or less demanding roles. Offering more flexible options can help older workers stay engaged while easing pressure on pension systems.

© UNFPA China Population ageing is a defining global trend of our time.

Cash for kids?

As birth rates fall, some governments react with cash bonuses, tax breaks, or even official fertility targets. The evidence suggests these measures have limited and short-lived impact.

“One-off payments don’t change long-term decisions,” says Mr. Herrmann. At best, they may influence when people have children, not whether they do.

UNFPA’s new Youth Reproductive Choices Survey, now under way in 70 countries, takes a different approach: asking people directly why they are having fewer children than they want.

Early results highlight a mix of economic and social pressures. High housing and childcare costs, insecure employment and worries about the future – from political instability to climate change – all weigh heavily.

So do unequal gender roles, with women often bearing most unpaid care and domestic work burdens.

“These are not issues that can be solved with a cheque,” Herrmann says.

The right to choose

Policies driven by fear of population decline can also undermine rights, particularly for women.

Fertility targets and top-down directives sometimes come with harmful assumptions; for example, that women should stay home, that sex education should be curtailed, or that access to reproductive healthcare should be restricted.

A rights-based approach starts from a different question: what prevents people from having the children they want?

From there, governments can identify practical solutions, such as affordable housing, accessible childcare, parental leave for both parents, stable jobs and equal pay. Such policies support families without coercion.

Ageing does not mean decline

Ageing populations do pose real challenges, especially for pension systems and health. But they do not automatically spell economic decline.

Spending on health and long-term care also creates jobs, particularly in services rooted in local communities. Older people, meanwhile, contribute in many ways beyond paid work, from caring for family members to volunteering.

The bigger challenge, Herrmann argues, is a smaller labour force. Addressing it requires inclusion – in other words, bringing more women, migrants, young people and older workers into employment – alongside investments that raise productivity, such as education, skills, technology, and infrastructure.

Migration is not a quick fix

Migration is another powerful – and often misunderstood – demographic force.

In countries experiencing steep population decline, low birth rates are usually only part of the story.

High emigration plays a major role too. In parts of the Western Balkans, populations have fallen by 20 to 30 per cent since the 1990s, largely because people left to seek work elsewhere.

By contrast, countries such as Germany have largely avoided population decline thanks to inward migration.

But migration is not a quick fix. Without language training, recognition of qualifications and pathways into work, many migrants remain excluded from the labour market to the detriment of both newcomers and host societies.

Listening instead of panicking

Ultimately, Mr. Herrmann’s vision of demographic resilience is grounded in listening.

If most people want two children but have fewer, the answer is not to pressure families, or panic. The solution involves understanding their realities and shaping policies that expand choice rather than limit it.

Get that right, he says, and demographic change becomes something societies can manage, with fairness, confidence and an eye on the long-term.
articles.source: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
articles.reasoning: The article addresses global demographic changes and promotes a positive, rights-based approach to managing lower fertility rates without economic decline. It highlights the importance of policies supporting individual choice, gender equality, economic security, and social inclusion, which have broad societal impact and offer a hopeful perspective on demographic challenges.
articles.personality_title: Lower birth rates don’t have to slow the economy, says UN expert
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Many countries around the world are seeing fewer babies being born and their populations are getting older. At the same time, many people want to have two or more children but end up having only one or none. This happens because of money worries, unfairness, and not enough support.

**What happened** – Michael Herrmann, an economist at the United Nations Population Fund (UNFPA), explained at a recent UN meeting that this change in population is not a crisis. Instead, he says we should understand it and find ways to adapt. He introduced the idea of "demographic resilience,” which means preparing societies to handle changes in population by improving education, health, and job skills.

**Impact** – This approach shows that even with fewer workers, countries can still grow by helping people work better and longer in ways that suit them. For example, older people can work part-time or do less tiring jobs. Also, giving money to families to have more children doesn’t usually work well because many other problems, like expensive housing and child care, stop people from having more kids.

**What’s next step** – Herrmann suggests that governments should listen carefully to what people need to have the families they want. This means making homes affordable, providing good child care, offering fair pay and jobs, and sharing care duties between men and women. These changes support families without forcing them to have more children.

**One-sentence takeaway** – By understanding people’s real needs and creating fair policies, countries can handle lower birth rates without hurting their economies or rights.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des taux de natalité plus bas ne signifient pas forcément un ralentissement économique, selon un expert de l’ONU
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Dans de nombreux pays, moins de bébés naissent et la population vieillit. Beaucoup de personnes souhaitent avoir deux enfants ou plus, mais finissent par en avoir un seul, voire aucun, à cause de problèmes financiers, d’inégalités et d’un manque de soutien.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Michael Herrmann, économiste au Fonds des Nations Unies pour la population (UNFPA), a expliqué lors d’une réunion récente de l’ONU que ce changement démographique n’est pas une crise. Il propose le concept de « résilience démographique », qui consiste à préparer les sociétés à ces changements en améliorant l’éducation, la santé et les compétences professionnelles.

**Impact** – Cette idée montre que même avec moins de travailleurs, les pays peuvent continuer à progresser en aidant les gens à travailler mieux et plus longtemps, selon leurs capacités. Par exemple, les personnes âgées peuvent travailler à temps partiel ou occuper des emplois moins fatigants. De plus, donner de l’argent aux familles pour avoir plus d’enfants ne suffit pas, car d’autres problèmes comme le coût élevé du logement et de la garde d’enfants les empêchent d’avoir plus d’enfants.

**Prochaine étape** – Herrmann conseille aux gouvernements d’écouter vraiment ce dont les gens ont besoin pour fonder la famille qu’ils souhaitent. Cela signifie rendre le logement abordable, offrir une bonne garde d’enfants, des emplois et des salaires justes, et partager les tâches de soins entre hommes et femmes. Ces mesures soutiennent les familles sans les forcer.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – En comprenant les besoins réels des personnes et en créant des politiques justes, les pays peuvent gérer la baisse des naissances sans nuire à leur économie ni aux droits des individus.
articles.personality_title_es: Las tasas de natalidad más bajas no tienen que frenar la economía, dice experto de la ONU
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – En muchos países, nacen menos bebés y la población envejece. Muchas personas quieren tener dos o más hijos, pero terminan teniendo uno o ninguno debido a problemas económicos, desigualdad y falta de apoyo.

**Qué pasó** – Michael Herrmann, economista del Fondo de Población de las Naciones Unidas (UNFPA), explicó en una reunión reciente de la ONU que este cambio demográfico no es una crisis. Propuso la idea de "resiliencia demográfica", que significa preparar a las sociedades para estos cambios mejorando la educación, la salud y las habilidades laborales.

**Impacto** – Esta idea muestra que, aunque haya menos trabajadores, los países pueden seguir creciendo ayudando a las personas a trabajar mejor y más tiempo, según sus capacidades. Por ejemplo, las personas mayores pueden trabajar a tiempo parcial o en trabajos menos exigentes. Además, dar dinero a las familias para que tengan más hijos no suele funcionar porque otros problemas, como la vivienda y el cuidado infantil caros, impiden que tengan más hijos.

**Próximo paso** – Herrmann sugiere que los gobiernos escuchen con atención qué necesitan las personas para tener la familia que desean. Esto implica viviendas asequibles, cuidado infantil accesible, empleos y salarios justos, y compartir las tareas de cuidado entre hombres y mujeres. Estas medidas apoyan a las familias sin obligarlas.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Al entender las necesidades reales de las personas y crear políticas justas, los países pueden manejar la baja natalidad sin dañar su economía ni los derechos de las personas.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a diverse, interconnected community garden where various sized seeds and sprouts symbolize different generations growing together, with flexible pathways winding through nurturing hands gently tending to young plants and mature trees—depicting demographic resilience, inclusive growth, and the nurturing of human potential amid changing population dynamics.

articles.title: The South Korean authors rising above a tide of hate to become bestsellers
articles.summary: A quiet revolution is unfolding as women writers carve out space in the wake of an anti-feminist backlash.
articles.content: It's hard to pin down a single motive that draws so many women - other than the fact that they are all seeking a room of their own, a place where they say they can find some freedom, a little bit of adventure, but most of all, as one of them put it, they can speak their mind feeling "safe and at ease".
articles.reasoning: The article highlights a significant social development where women writers in South Korea are overcoming anti-feminist backlash to claim their space and freedom of expression. This represents progress in gender equality and freedom of speech, impacting society broadly by challenging harmful norms and empowering marginalized voices.
articles.personality_title: South Korean women writers rise to bestseller status despite backlash
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – In South Korea, there has been a strong anti-feminist backlash, making it difficult for women to express their views freely. Many women writers face criticism and hostility as they try to share their stories.

**What happened** – Despite this negative environment, a growing number of South Korean women authors have become bestsellers. They are creating a space where they feel safe to speak their minds and explore new ideas through their writing.

**Impact** – This rise is important because it shows women reclaiming their freedom of expression and challenging unfair attitudes. Their success helps change how society sees women’s voices and encourages more open conversations about gender and equality.

**What's next step** – As more women writers gain recognition, they may inspire others to write and speak out. This movement could lead to greater acceptance of diverse perspectives and stronger support for gender equality in South Korea.

**One-sentence takeaway** – South Korean women authors are breaking barriers to become bestselling voices despite facing strong opposition.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les auteures sud-coréennes deviennent best-sellers malgré l’opposition
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – En Corée du Sud, un fort mouvement anti-féministe rend difficile l’expression libre des femmes. De nombreuses écrivaines sont critiquées et confrontées à l’hostilité lorsqu’elles veulent partager leurs histoires.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Malgré cette situation négative, un nombre croissant d’auteures sud-coréennes sont devenues best-sellers. Elles créent un espace où elles se sentent en sécurité pour exprimer leurs idées et explorer de nouveaux sujets à travers leurs écrits.

**Impact** – Cette montée en puissance est importante car elle montre que les femmes reprennent leur liberté d’expression et défient les attitudes injustes. Leur succès aide à changer la façon dont la société perçoit les voix féminines et encourage plus de discussions ouvertes sur le genre et l’égalité.

**Prochaine étape** – À mesure que plus d’écrivaines seront reconnues, elles pourraient inspirer d’autres femmes à écrire et à s’exprimer. Ce mouvement pourrait mener à une meilleure acceptation des perspectives diverses et à un soutien renforcé pour l’égalité des sexes en Corée du Sud.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les auteures sud-coréennes brisent les barrières pour devenir des voix best-sellers malgré une forte opposition.
articles.personality_title_es: Las escritoras surcoreanas alcanzan el éxito a pesar de la oposición
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – En Corea del Sur, un fuerte movimiento antifeminista dificulta que las mujeres expresen libremente sus ideas. Muchas escritoras enfrentan críticas y hostilidad al intentar contar sus historias.

**Qué pasó** – A pesar de este ambiente negativo, un número creciente de autoras surcoreanas se han convertido en bestsellers. Están creando un espacio donde se sienten seguras para expresar sus pensamientos y explorar nuevas ideas a través de sus libros.

**Impacto** – Este avance es importante porque muestra que las mujeres recuperan su libertad de expresión y desafían actitudes injustas. Su éxito ayuda a cambiar cómo la sociedad ve las voces femeninas y fomenta conversaciones más abiertas sobre género e igualdad.

**Próximo paso** – A medida que más escritoras sean reconocidas, podrían inspirar a otras a escribir y expresarse. Este movimiento podría conducir a una mayor aceptación de perspectivas diversas y un mayor apoyo a la igualdad de género en Corea del Sur.

**Resumen en una frase** – Las escritoras surcoreanas están rompiendo barreras para convertirse en voces exitosas a pesar de enfrentar una fuerte oposición.
articles.image_prompt: A cozy, warmly lit room with an open window revealing a calm sky, where floating pages of handwritten manuscripts gently rise like birds, symbolizing South Korean women authors finding their own space to freely express their voices amid a peaceful, supportive atmosphere.

articles.title: 'We're not teaching them to order baguettes' - languages enjoy a renaissance in schools
articles.summary: After a long decline, the number of pupils taking language GCSEs in Wales rises for two years.
articles.content: "It's not so you can order a baguette in a boulangerie. It's so that you can understand that other people have different perspectives to you and they see things, feel things, taste things, experience in a really broad way their whole world in a different way," said Lucy.
articles.reasoning: The article highlights a meaningful increase in language learning among pupils in Wales, which promotes cultural understanding and empathy—key aspects of human rights and social cohesion. The rise in language GCSE uptake after a long decline represents a positive educational and societal trend with broad impact.
articles.personality_title: Language learning rises again in Welsh schools after years of decline
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – For many years, fewer students in Wales chose to study languages in school. This decline worried teachers and experts because learning languages helps people understand different cultures and ideas.

**What happened** – Recently, the number of pupils taking language GCSE exams in Wales has increased for two years in a row. GCSEs are important exams students take around age 16. This change shows more young people are interested in learning new languages again.

**Impact** – Learning languages is not just about speaking another tongue or ordering food in a foreign shop. It helps students see the world from different points of view and understand how others live and feel. This kind of learning supports kindness, respect, and better communication between people from different backgrounds.

**What's next step** – Schools and teachers hope this positive trend will continue. They plan to encourage even more students to take language classes and show how language skills connect to real-life experiences and global understanding.

**One-sentence takeaway** – More Welsh students are choosing to learn languages, helping them connect with others and see the world differently.
articles.personality_title_fr: L'apprentissage des langues repart à la hausse dans les écoles galloises après des années de baisse
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Pendant de nombreuses années, moins d'élèves au Pays de Galles choisissaient d'étudier les langues à l'école. Cette baisse inquiétait les enseignants et les experts car apprendre des langues aide à comprendre différentes cultures et idées.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Récemment, le nombre d'élèves passant les examens GCSE de langues au Pays de Galles a augmenté pendant deux années consécutives. Les GCSE sont des examens importants passés vers 16 ans. Ce changement montre que davantage de jeunes s'intéressent de nouveau à l'apprentissage des langues.

**Impact** – Apprendre une langue ne sert pas seulement à parler ou à commander dans un magasin étranger. Cela aide les élèves à voir le monde sous différents angles et à comprendre comment vivent et ressentent les autres. Ce type d'apprentissage favorise la gentillesse, le respect et une meilleure communication entre personnes de milieux différents.

**Étape suivante** – Les écoles et les enseignants espèrent que cette tendance positive va continuer. Ils prévoient d'encourager encore plus d'élèves à suivre des cours de langues et à montrer comment ces compétences sont utiles dans la vie et pour comprendre le monde.

**En une phrase** – De plus en plus d'élèves gallois choisissent d'apprendre des langues, ce qui les aide à se connecter aux autres et à voir le monde autrement.
articles.personality_title_es: El aprendizaje de idiomas vuelve a crecer en las escuelas galesas tras años de descenso
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Durante muchos años, menos estudiantes en Gales elegían estudiar idiomas en la escuela. Esta disminución preocupaba a profesores y expertos porque aprender idiomas ayuda a entender diferentes culturas e ideas.

**Qué pasó** – Recientemente, el número de alumnos que hacen los exámenes GCSE de idiomas en Gales ha aumentado durante dos años seguidos. Los GCSE son exámenes importantes que los estudiantes hacen alrededor de los 16 años. Este cambio muestra que más jóvenes están interesados en aprender nuevos idiomas otra vez.

**Impacto** – Aprender idiomas no es solo para hablar o pedir comida en una tienda extranjera. Ayuda a los estudiantes a ver el mundo desde diferentes puntos de vista y a entender cómo viven y sienten otras personas. Este tipo de aprendizaje fomenta la amabilidad, el respeto y una mejor comunicación entre personas de diferentes orígenes.

**Próximo paso** – Las escuelas y los profesores esperan que esta tendencia positiva continúe. Planean animar a más estudiantes a tomar clases de idiomas y mostrar cómo estas habilidades se relacionan con experiencias reales y el entendimiento global.

**Frase clave** – Más estudiantes galeses están eligiendo aprender idiomas, lo que les ayuda a conectarse con otros y a ver el mundo de forma diferente.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of diverse, colorful speech bubbles gently intertwining and flowing like ribbons around an open book, set against a soft, natural background of layered paper textures and subtle earth tones, symbolizing the rich, empathetic understanding and broad perspectives gained through learning languages.
